summary.overall: Chapter 9 was reviewed in 11 bounded source-grounded shards. Proposed changes: 161 replacements, 1 splits, and 19 removals.
patterns: Paragraph-location scaffolding is the primary defect, affecting all 15 questions uniformly. Stems open with 'Under 9-X-Y' and explanations cite paragraph numbers, teaching document trivia rather than operational substance.
True/false explanations for flagged items (eb7dd2fc, e3489418, 144eafba, 7ac08459) are thin — they state only that the rule exists in a paragraph number without teaching the operational content.
Two flashcards contain source-fidelity errors: cc5c48e7 weakens 'will require reinitiation' to 'usually requires,' and afe263d5 replaces 'amount of delay to expect' with 'why it is needed.'

Activities are well-constructed with self-contained stems, plausible distractors, and accurate content. The paragraph references in their explanations are informational citations rather than scaffolding defects.
Cross-format reinforcement exists for SFRA requirements (question e234627d, activity fc9ad2c0, flashcard 7492e893 all cover the three requirements for DC SFRA operations), which is appropriate.
Exact phraseology for phraseology blocks (9-2-10) is not tested in any item — the shard tests the operational rules but not the prescribed wording.

Paragraph-location scaffolding: 17 of 24 items embed paragraph references in stems, explanations, or para_context fields. The automated_flags heuristics correctly identified these. Stems like 'Under 9-2-11, what must...' teach students to associate answers with paragraph numbers rather than operational situations.

Thin explanations: All three true/false questions and two multiple-choice questions have explanations that are either bare paragraph citations ('That requirement is stated in 9-2-11') or one-line restatements without operational context.

Answer-length cue: Question 7202a9c7 has a correct answer that is three times the length of any distractor because it concatenates the three sub-requirements from 9-2-12b4. The correct answer is visually distinguishable by length alone.

Duplicated retrieval: Activity 4befd3f3 (knowledge_check) and question 5617cafb both test the same MARSA-begin concept via multiple-choice format. One should be redirected to a different MARSA sub-topic such as MARSA termination.
Flashcard quality: All four flashcards are well-scoped, free of paragraph scaffolding, and concise. They demonstrate the standard that other formats should meet.

False positive on negative_stem heuristic: Several items were flagged 'negative_stem' because the source text uses conditional negatives ('not known,' 'must not compromise'). These are legitimate source conditions, not manufactured negative stems.

Paragraph-location scaffolding: 19 of 22 items contain paragraph references in stems, explanations, or para_context fields. The automated_flags heuristics correctly identified these on 17 items (two unflagged items also had paragraph-location explanations). Stems like 'Under 9-2-15, when may...' teach students to associate answers with paragraph numbers rather than operational situations.

Thin explanations on true/false: All four T/F items (dc5a9540, 78265f16, d758943c, 8a639a62) have explanations that are bare paragraph citations ('That restriction is stated in 9-2-15c') or near-equivalents rather than teaching the controlling rule. This is a consistent defect across every T/F item in the shard.

Paragraph-location explanations on MCQs and activities: An additional 11 items have explanations beginning with 'Per 9-2-X' that restate the answer while prefixed with document trivia. The remaining items had thinner issues.

Duplicated retrieval: Activity 6a8f87b1 (knowledge_check) and question cc8ea235 both test the same 9-2-16a concept (advise + reroute on request for hazardous-radiation traverse) via near-identical multiple-choice format. This is the same retrieval mode testing the same source statement.

Flashcard quality divergence: The flashcard for 9-2-15 (c98f9926) has a circular back ('Assign them to the listed qualifying military operations') that fails to enumerate the retrieval target, while the three other flashcards are well-scoped, scaffolding-free, and effective. This is a single-card defect rather than a systemic pattern.

False positive on negative_stem heuristic: Items a4b2b4fa, dc5a9540, 593a2a2d, 8a639a62, and f1ed2013 were flagged 'negative_stem' because the source text uses negative or restrictive language ('do not assign,' 'except where'). These are legitimate source-required restrictions, not manufactured negative stems. The negative imperatives in T/F stems (8a639a62: 'must not be delayed') are the actual operational rule being tested.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 18 of 19 items (all questions and activities) embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or activity prompts. This teaches document navigation instead of operational substance.
Four true/false items test verbatim recall of isolated facts with thin explanations. Converting to multiple-choice strengthens retrieval by requiring discrimination among plausible distractors.
Answer-length cue detected in two dropsonde-advisory items where the correct choice is substantially longer and more detailed than distractors, leaking the answer independent of content knowledge.

Explanations are generally accurate but many simply restate the answer without connecting to the operational context or the responsible party's role (e.g., aircraft commander's delay decision after receiving advisory).
Flashcards across all four paragraphs are clean with no defects. They capture the core retrieval targets concisely.

Cross-format reinforcement exists but is thin: the dropsonde advisory is covered by question, activity, and flashcard, but other procedures (e.g., celestial nav control transfer, evasive action approval basis) appear in only one or two formats.
Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding in stems (7 of 12 questions open with 'Under 9-2-X') and explanations across questions and activities
Four true/false items restate source text verbatim rather than requiring operational reasoning; each is redundant with a stronger multiple_choice or activity item on the same concept
One knowledge_check activity (f53f8f89) duplicates the retrieval target of a multiple_choice question (1c33f2a9) without adding distinct format value
Thin explanations that cite paragraph numbers ('Per 9-2-3a2', 'That requirement is stated in 9-2-5c') instead of teaching the controlling operational principle
All four flashcards are clean, self-contained, correctly scoped, and require no intervention — the strongest format in this shard
Activity formats (situation_action, knowledge_check) are well-chosen for their learning objectives once scaffolding is removed

Paragraph-location scaffolding: 10 of 12 questions and 4 of 6 activities open stems, explanations, or para_context with paragraph citations that teach document navigation instead of operational reasoning.
Thin true/false items: 3 T/F questions restate source text verbatim with explanations that only cite the paragraph number, producing trivial recognition tasks.
Near-duplication within format: b4c0cc1b (T/F) duplicates the concept tested by 93b7d553 (MC) in the same entity type; 71362d90 (T/F) overlaps substantively with 04529d7f (MC).
Cross-reference as testable content: b753851e tests whether the reader knows that offshore/oceanic operations follow Chapter 8, which is a pointer to other material rather than an actionable rule.
Overloaded flashcard: defe75d8 packs four exception conditions into one card back, undermining focused retrieval.
Answer-length cue: activity 60d516e6 has a correct answer of 28 words against distractors of 12-15 words, telegraphing the answer.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect: 10 of 12 question stems, most explanations, and two activity para_context fields embed paragraph numbers instead of standing alone on operational substance.
True/false format is overused for narrow source-verification checks; three of four true/false items have thin explanations ("That statement appears in the note to 9-X-Y") that do not teach the rule.
One true/false question (af17f5fc) restates the full text of paragraph 9-4-1 verbatim, duplicating content already tested by two multiple-choice questions on the same paragraph.
A single multiple-choice correct answer in bb01dec7 is three times the length of its distractors, creating an answer-length cue.
One knowledge-check stem (fdc19a10) adds the word 'first,' implying a prioritization order that is absent from the source paragraph.
Activity variety is a strength: readback_check, spot_the_error, situation_action, and knowledge_check formats provide distinct retrieval modes.

Footnote and cross-reference content from source notes (FAA jurisdiction limits, VFR traffic permission for stationary ALTRVs) is tested but in weak true/false formats that should be elevated to operational scenarios.
The single-sentence paragraph 9-4-1 is over-represented with three questions and one activity for what is essentially one retrieval target.
Paragraph-location scaffolding in stems and explanations is the dominant defect across all four paragraphs.
Three verbatim true/false items (one per source paragraph in 9-4-2, 9-4-3, 9-4-5) duplicate the corresponding multiple-choice questions without adding retrieval diversity.
Explanations in scaffolded items tend to restate the answer rather than teach the full operational rule, though source content is factually correct.
The readback_check activity for 9-4-5 phraseology is a format mismatch: broadcast advisories are not read back by pilots.
Four flashcards are concise, correctly typed, and free of scaffolding or error. No splits are needed — each item tests a single retrieval target.
Distractors across all items represent plausible operational mistakes rather than obviously wrong answers.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 13 question stems and 6 activity para_context fields open with 'Under 9-X-Y', and 14 explanations begin with 'Per 9-X-Y'. Students do not need to memorize paragraph numbers; the operational rule is what matters.
Thin true/false explanations: two items (df42a944, e69b177f) explain only by citing a subparagraph letter rather than stating the procedure being tested.

Single-format redundancy: the true/false 3ea6eeb1 duplicates the civil-operator written-agreement condition already assessed by multiple-choice questions c305199f and 6567b17d within the same paragraph. Three items in one format testing one narrow rule is excessive.

Cross-format reinforcement exists but is sparse: the 1-hour advance-notice requirement (9-6-1) and the 10-minute derelict-balloon broadcast (9-6-2) each appear in two formats, but most source statements appear in only one format.

Distractor quality is a strength: incorrect choices typically represent plausible operational errors (e.g. assuming verbal liability acceptance substitutes for a written agreement, assuming charted drop areas authorize all aircraft, assuming standardized climb rates permit vertical separation).
Situation-action activities provide operational context that other formats lack, testing the application of rules to realistic scenarios.
Paragraph-location scaffolding is universal: all 12 question stems and all 5 activity explanations prefix with the paragraph number, teaching document navigation instead of ATC procedure.
Four true/false items are near-verbatim source quotes with inadequate explanations, producing trivial recognition checks that duplicate content covered in better-formatted multiple-choice items.
The knowledge-check activity for 9-7-1 (61521ba1) carries a significant answer-length cue: the correct answer is substantially longer than all three distractors.
Flashcards across all four paragraphs have precise, recall-appropriate front/back phrasing with no scaffolding or format defects.
The two situation-action activities (9-7-1) present plausible operational dilemmas and use distractors that reflect common misconceptions about coordination timing.
No items invent unsupported rationales or add safety claims absent from the source blocks.
Paragraph-location scaffolding in two question stems ('Under 9-8-1,') and two explanations ('Per 9-8-1,'), teaching paragraph-number recall instead of operational procedure.

Verbatim true/false recognition: item f57bc6d7 restates the source directive as a true statement with a thin explanation naming the paragraph, testing trivial recognition rather than operational understanding.

Format-level redundancy: the knowledge-check activity (df3640ec) asks the same question as the flashcard (f1f36d65)—'what must be done with UAP activity'—producing duplicate retrieval targets across formats.

Multi-item redundancy for a single-sentence source: three questions, one activity, and one flashcard all target the same single directive, with the true/false and activity adding no distinct retrieval dimension.
The flashcard is the strongest item: a focused front question and concise back answer with no scaffolding, format defects, or extraneous content.
generation_guidance: Stems must be self-contained. Never begin a stem with 'Under 9-X-Y' or any paragraph-number reference. Identify the operational situation, not the document location.
Explanations must teach the operational rule, condition, or exception. Do not merely cite a paragraph number. State what the controller must do and the circumstances under which the rule applies.

Do not invent reasons that are absent from the source blocks. If the source states only a duty or requirement, explain that duty directly. If the source provides a reason (e.g., 'carefully selected routes' for dangerous-materials flights), incorporate it.

Flashcard backs must match source wording precisely for facts, thresholds, and definitions. For synthesized procedure cards, verify each claim against the source. Never weaken mandatory language ('will require') with hedges ('usually requires').
Maintain balanced answer length across choices. When the correct answer is a compound rule, write distractors that are comparably detailed to avoid length-cue leakage.
For true/false items, write explanations that restate the rule in full rather than pointing to a paragraph location. The explanation is the primary teaching moment.

Do not generate activity items that duplicate the same question stem and answer set as an existing question. Cross-format reinforcement should test the concept from a different angle or with different distractors, not replicate the item.

Test exact phraseology where it is prescribed in phraseology blocks. The DC SFRA paragraph (9-2-10) contains multiple prescribed phraseology entries that are not reflected in any flashcard or question in this shard.
Do not prefix question stems with 'Under 9-2-X' or equivalent paragraph identifiers. Write stems that describe the operational situation or ask directly about the controller duty.

Do not begin explanations with 'Per 9-2-X.' Teach the rule, condition, or exception using the source's own operational language. Cite paragraph numbers only in reference metadata, not in learner-facing text.

For paragraphs with multiple distinct sub-requirements (e.g., 9-2-12b4), test each requirement in a separate item rather than concatenating them into one answer choice. Combined-list correct answers create length cues.
True/false items must have explanations that teach the controlling rule, not merely state where in the order the rule appears.

When a paragraph has several sub-topics (e.g., 9-2-13 covers MARSA begin, MARSA end, tanker responsibilities, squawk handling, altitude vacating, comm failure), distribute items across sub-topics before duplicating retrieval of the same sub-topic in multiple formats.

Readback-check activities should use prescribed phraseology exactly as written in the source. Spot-the-error activities are well-suited for testing exact phraseology. Use situation-action for operational decision-making scenarios.
Knowledge-check activities that are essentially multiple-choice questions should test concepts not already covered by the question pool for the same paragraph.

Do not prefix question stems with 'Under 9-2-X' or equivalent paragraph identifiers. Write stems that describe the operational situation or ask directly about the controller duty without document navigation cues.

Do not begin explanations with 'Per 9-2-X.' Teach the controlling rule, condition, or exception using the source's own operational language. Paragraph numbers belong in reference metadata, not learner-facing text.

True/false explanations must teach the rule, not merely state which paragraph contains it. For T/F items, the explanation should confirm the rule and provide enough operational context to distinguish it from nearby requirements.

Do not reference internal paragraph structure ('subparagraph a1,' 'subparagraph a2') in question stems. These references require the student to memorize paragraph layout rather than operational content.

When a knowledge-check activity tests the exact same source statement as an existing multiple-choice question, redirect the activity to a different sub-topic or use a different activity type (e.g., situation_action) that exercises a distinct retrieval skill.

Flashcard backs must enumerate the retrieval target explicitly. A back that says 'the listed qualifying military operations' without listing them is circular and fails to teach. For list-based recall where the complete list is the target, include the full enumeration.
For paragraphs with paired rules (e.g., 9-2-16a for en route traverse and 9-2-16b for destination airfield), distribute items across both sub-rules rather than double-testing the same one.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or activity content; reference the operational context (aircraft type, airspace, procedure name) instead.

For true/false items, convert any that test a standalone fact into multiple-choice questions that require discriminating the correct answer from operationally plausible distractors. Reserve true/false for genuinely binary judgments such as 'is this phraseology correct?'
Balance choice lengths so the correct answer does not stand out by verbosity or specificity. Each distractor should be as detailed and plausible as the correct answer.

Activity explanations must teach the operational principle, not merely restate the correct answer. Connect the answer to the controller's duty, the pilot's responsibility, or the controlling condition from the source.
Prefer operational situation-action activities that require applying a rule to a realistic scenario over knowledge-check activities that quiz verbatim recall of introductory sentences.

Expand cross-format coverage: each procedurally significant rule (e.g., celestial nav control-transfer briefing, nonstandard-formation breakup clearance) should appear in at least two of question/activity/flashcard.

Eliminate all paragraph-location scaffolding from stems and explanations in future generation passes; no stem should begin with 'Under 9-X-Y' and no explanation should cite a paragraph number as its primary teaching device

Replace verbatim true/false restatements with multiple_choice items that require operational application of the rule, or omit the target entirely if the concept is already adequately covered by another item

Ensure knowledge_check activities test a distinctly different retrieval mode or cognitive skill than the corresponding multiple_choice question; do not generate both formats for the same single-answer retrieval target
Write explanations that teach the controlling operational principle directly — state the duty, threshold, condition, or procedural requirement without routing the learner through a paragraph citation

Maintain the flashcard generation quality observed in this shard: self-contained fronts that identify the concept, concise backs scoped to one retrieval target, and operational phrasing without paragraph references

Never open a stem or explanation with 'Under 9-X-Y' or 'Per 9-X-Y.' Write self-contained stems that identify the operational situation or rule and explanations that teach the controlling principle without citing paragraph coordinates.

Use true/false sparingly and only when the proposition is genuinely contestable. Do not restate a source sentence verbatim and ask whether it is true — the answer is always 'true' and the task teaches nothing.

When a concept appears in both question and activity formats, vary the tested aspect rather than retesting the identical fact. Cross-format reinforcement is valuable; same-format near-duplication is waste.

Do not test cross-references, CFR part citations, or 'where is this found' trivia as standalone items. If a cross-reference is important, embed it in an operational scenario that requires applying the referenced procedure.
Keep flashcard backs to a single retrieval target. When a paragraph lists multiple conditions or exceptions, split them across separate cards keyed to distinct fronts.

Balance choice lengths so the correct answer does not stand out by word count against shorter distractors. When the correct rule is nuanced and needs more words, add specificity to distractors to close the length gap.

Situation-action activities in this shard are well executed: concrete scenarios, plausible operational distractors, and explanations that tie the answer back to the controlling rule. Use them as the template for future activity generation.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems or activity prompts. Frame the operational situation or regulatory condition and let the learner identify the correct action or rule.
For true/false items, write explanations that teach the controlling principle or operational consequence, not that 'this statement appears in the text.'

Avoid creating multiple items that test the same source statement in the same retrieval mode. A short paragraph like 9-4-1 needs at most one question and one card, not three questions and an activity.
Ensure multiple-choice correct answers are comparable in length to distractors. When the rule text itself is long, distill it to its operational core rather than quoting a full sentence.

When a source rule cross-references another paragraph (e.g., 'clear the aircraft in accordance with paragraph 9-3-2'), educational items should describe what the cross-referenced action means operationally, not test recall of the paragraph number.

Activity para_context fields should state the relevant rule in plain language without paragraph citations. Cross-references to other source locations should be translated into their operational meaning.
Flashcard backs should be complete: include fallback conditions and contingency actions when the source provides them.

Explanations must state the controlling requirement, condition, or exception directly from the source blocks. Do not add inferred safety rationales, policy purposes, or operational consequences the source does not explicitly state.
Never begin stems with 'Under 9-X-Y' or explanations with 'Per 9-X-Y.' Write self-contained items that reference the operational situation, not the document location.

Do not generate true/false items that are verbatim reproductions of a source paragraph. Use T/F only when testing a specific, non-obvious operational distinction that is not covered by an existing multiple-choice question.
For paragraphs with prescribed phraseology (e.g., 9-4-5), use activities that test recognition of the correct phraseology structure. Do not frame broadcast advisories as readback exercises.

Explanations must teach the full operational context: for 9-4-4, state all four IFR separation methods, not just the one the question targets. For 9-4-2, state the rule and its exception rather than just confirming the answer.
When a paragraph contains a multi-element list or minima (9-4-4, 9-4-5), ensure coverage across formats includes at least one item that tests the complete set, not just isolated values.
Flashcard quality in this shard is strong. Continue the pattern: clear conceptual front, concise operational back, appropriate card_type.
Write question stems that are self-contained without paragraph-number prefixes. The controlling rule should be identifiable from the stem alone.

Write explanations that teach the operational rule, condition, or exception — not the paragraph location. If the explanation cannot state the controlling principle without citing a paragraph number, the item may be testing trivia rather than substance.

Limit same-format items per narrow rule. A single well-constructed multiple-choice question often covers the same ground as a true/false item plus a second multiple-choice question on the same condition.

True/false items benefit from explanations that confirm what the procedure is, not merely which subparagraph it appears in. If the explanation reduces to a paragraph citation, rewrite it to teach the procedure.

The readback_check format works for advisory phraseology recognition even though advisories are not formally read back — the skill tested is correct-wording identification. The format is acceptable provided the explanation teaches what the phraseology conveys.

For visual_interpretation activities, referencing the figure by its official label (e.g. FIG 9-6-1) is necessary context, not paragraph-location scaffolding. The question should drive a lookup or interpretation decision, not merely ask what the figure number is.
Continue producing situation-action activities with realistic operational dilemmas. These are the strongest items in the shard for teaching rule application.
Write every stem and explanation in operational terms: state the airspace class, controller action, or operational condition without prefixing a paragraph or section number.
Avoid true/false items that restate source text verbatim. Reserve true/false for distinctions that require fine operational judgment (e.g., airspace class differences, conditional applicability).

Match distractor length to the correct-answer length in knowledge-check activities. When the correct answer is necessarily detailed, expand distractors to comparable length with plausible but incorrect operational details.

The four flashcards in this shard are a good benchmark for single-target retrieval cards. Follow the same pattern for future generation: a focused front question and a concise back answer listing required elements without paragraph references.

Do not generate paragraph-location items such as 'Under 9-7-4...' or 'Per 9-7-2...'. Frame every item around the airspace, controller duty, or operational condition so the student learns the rule, not its address.

For single-sentence paragraphs, generate at most two complementary multiple-choice items (testing distinct operational dimensions) and one flashcard. Do not generate true/false items that restate the source verbatim or activities that duplicate existing retrieval targets.
Write every stem and explanation in operational terms without prefixing a paragraph or section number. State the controller duty or operational condition directly.

Reserve true/false for distinctions requiring fine operational judgment (e.g., conditional applicability, exceptions to a rule). Do not use true/false to test recognition of a single-sentence directive.

Before generating a knowledge-check activity, verify that the question and retrieval target differ from both the flashcard front and the multiple-choice stems already generated for the same paragraph.
The flashcard in this shard is a good benchmark: a focused front question and a concise back answer with no paragraph references. Follow this pattern for single-procedure cards.
shard_overviews: The shard covers five paragraphs (9-1-1, 9-1-2, 9-1-3, 9-2-1, 9-2-10) with 23 target items. Content accuracy is good — all 15 questions, 3 activities, and 5 flashcards accurately reflect the source blocks with no unsafe operational implications. The dominant defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: all 15 questions open with 'Under 9-X-Y' and cite paragraph numbers in explanations, substituting document navigation for operational context. Two flashcards (cc5c48e7 for recorded run, afe263d5 for dangerous-materials restrictions) contain factual fidelity errors in their backs. The three activities are self-contained and accurate. No items required removal or splitting.

The shard covers four dense paragraphs (SECNOT, law enforcement/sensitive missions, aerial refueling, and FL600+ operations) with 24 items. Factual accuracy is solid across all items — no source-fidelity errors were found. However, pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding in stems and explanations degrades educational coherence: nearly every question stem is prefixed with 'Under 9-2-X' and nearly every explanation begins with 'Per 9-2-X,' teaching document trivia rather than operational substance. True/false items have explanations that merely cite a paragraph number rather than teaching the rule. One question (7202a9c7) packs three distinct requirements into a single answer choice, creating a length cue that reveals the correct answer. One knowledge-check activity (4befd3f3) duplicates the MARSA-begin concept already tested by question 5617cafb in a near-identical MCQ format. The four flashcards are well-constructed, focused, and free of defects.

This shard covers four paragraphs (9-2-15 through 9-2-18) on military special use frequencies, nuclear radiation avoidance, SAMP flights, and AWACS/NORAD Special flights. All 22 items are factually accurate and source-aligned — no source-fidelity errors were found. However, paragraph-location scaffolding is pervasive: 19 of 22 items embed 'Under 9-2-X' in stems or 'Per 9-2-X' in explanations. All four true/false questions have explanations that are bare paragraph citations rather than operational teaching. One flashcard has a circular back that fails to enumerate the retrieval target. One knowledge-check activity near-duplicates a multiple-choice question testing the same 9-2-16a concept in the same retrieval mode. The three kept flashcards (9-2-16, 9-2-17, 9-2-18) and both situation-action activities are well-constructed aside from paragraph scaffolding in their para_context fields.

This shard covers four special-operations paragraphs (9-2-19, 9-2-2, 9-2-20, 9-2-21) with generally accurate factual content but pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding that contaminates stems, explanations, and activity prompts. Every question and activity required replacement primarily to remove paragraph-number references; four true/false items were converted to multiple-choice for stronger retrieval. The four flashcards are sound with no defects. Source fidelity is high across all targets: no item tested incorrect facts or manufactured information. Distractors are plausible and stem from recognizable operational misunderstandings. The dropsonde, celestial-navigation, evasive-action, and nonstandard-formation items cover the operational essentials from each paragraph.

Shard 5 covers four special-operations paragraphs (9-2-3 through 9-2-6) with mostly accurate source fidelity but pervasive presentation defects. The primary issue is paragraph-location scaffolding — 7 of 12 questions open with 'Under 9-2-X' and many explanations cite paragraph numbers instead of teaching the operational rule. Four true/false items are verbatim source-text restatements that test recognition rather than operational application; each duplicates a concept already covered by a stronger multiple_choice question or activity. One knowledge_check activity mirrors a multiple_choice question on the same retrieval target without adding format diversity. All four flashcards are sound, self-contained, and require no intervention. After remediation: 8 question replacements remove paragraph scaffolding, 3 activity replacements fix paragraph-location explanations, 4 true/false verbatim items are removed, and 1 redundant activity is removed.

The shard contains factually accurate content drawn faithfully from the source paragraphs, with no source-fidelity or unsafe-implication defects. The dominant and pervasive issue is paragraph-location scaffolding: nearly every question stem and explanation opens with 'Under 9-2-X' or 'Per 9-2-X,' teaching document navigation instead of operational substance. Three true/false items are trivial verbatim restatements with thin explanations that cite only the paragraph location; two are near-duplicates of existing multiple-choice items and one tests a cross-reference. One flashcard is overloaded with four distinct exception conditions in a single retrieval target. One activity has a correct answer substantially longer than its distractors, creating a length cue. The activity situation_action items are well-crafted with plausible operational distractors and represent the strongest format in the shard.

This shard covers four well-scoped paragraphs (9-3-2 through 9-4-1) with largely accurate content. The primary defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: nearly every question stem begins with 'Under 9-X-Y' and explanations cite paragraph numbers instead of teaching the controlling operational rule. Three true/false questions (cf7b9466, 63accddf, 37be0248) test narrow verbatim recall with explanations that merely confirm the statement appears in the text rather than teaching the principle. One true/false (af17f5fc) completely duplicates content from two multiple-choice questions on the same short paragraph, contributing no new retrieval value. Activity quality is stronger — the readback check (c48d7e6a) and spot-the-error (7f4d8e95) are well-constructed, and the situation-action scenarios provide meaningful decision practice once para_context citations are cleaned. Flashcards are generally concise and accurate, with one missing the fallback contingency from the source.

The shard covers four short paragraphs (9-4-2 through 9-4-5) with 21 items. Content accuracy is sound — no source-fidelity errors were found. However, paragraph-location scaffolding is pervasive: nearly every question stem begins with 'Under 9-4-X' and explanations start with 'Per 9-4-X.' Three questions are verbatim true/false reproductions of source paragraphs with explanations that merely identify the paragraph number; these teach nothing beyond document recognition. One activity misuses the readback_check format for a broadcast advisory that pilots do not read back. The four flashcards are clean and require no intervention. The remaining items cover distinct operational concepts (routing exception, altitude minima, separation methods, advisory intervals, phraseology) and just need scaffolding removed and explanations strengthened.

The shard covers three short paragraphs (9-5-1 Jettisoning of External Stores, 9-6-1 Unmanned Free Balloons Application, 9-6-2 Derelict Balloons). Source fidelity is uniformly high — every item correctly reflects its source block. The dominant defect is paragraph-location scaffolding: 17 of 21 items embed "Under 9-X-Y" in stems or "Per 9-X-Y" in explanations. Two true/false items have explanations that merely cite a subparagraph letter without teaching the procedure. One true/false question in 9-5-1 is a verbatim restatement of the civil-operator written-agreement rule already tested by two stronger items in the same shard. The three flashcards are clean and require no intervention. The activities span multiple types — knowledge_check, situation_action, readback_check, spot_the_error, and visual_interpretation — and all align with their respective skills. Distractors are generally plausible operational mistakes rather than obvious throwaways.

The shard covers four paragraphs (9-7-1 through 9-7-4) spanning parachute-jump coordination rules across airspace classes. Every multiple-choice question, knowledge-check activity, and true/false item embeds paragraph-location scaffolding in its stem or explanation, teaching students to recall paragraph numbers rather than the operational rules. Four true/false questions across the shard are verbatim restatements of source text with one-sentence explanations, testing trivial recognition rather than operational understanding and duplicating content already covered by more substantive items. The four flashcards are clean, well-scoped retrieval targets with no scaffolding or format defects. Two activities present genuine operational situations and integrate source context well beyond the scaffolding issue. No items introduce unsupported safety rationales or invent content absent from the source blocks.

The shard covers a single paragraph (9-8-1) containing one directive: inform the operations supervisor/CIC of any UAP or unexplained phenomena activity. The paragraph is a single sentence, yet the targets stretch it across three questions, one activity, and one flashcard—producing substantial redundancy across formats. Two multiple-choice questions test complementary dimensions of the rule (trigger condition and recipient) using plausible distractors but embed paragraph-location scaffolding in stems and explanations. One true/false question restates the source text verbatim with a single-sentence explanation naming the paragraph, adding no educational value beyond recognition of a direct quote. The knowledge-check activity duplicates the flashcard's retrieval target verbatim (what must be done with UAP activity) while also overlapping the content tested by the two multiple-choice questions. Provide any assistance requested even if it requires compromising approved separation minima, because sensitive government missions override standard ATC safety limits.

content.explanation: Per 9-2-12, ATC does not change sensitive beacon codes or alter special call signs for sensitive government missions, and assistance to law enforcement aircraft cannot compromise approved separation or safety.

content.situation: An IFR aircraft is approaching an active MOA. The pilot advises that the using agency has already given permission to operate in the airspace, but another controller still wants to apply special-use-airspace avoidance procedures as if no exception exists.
content.para_context: Special use airspace procedures apply unless an exception exists. Exceptions include: the pilot informs ATC that permission has been obtained from the using agency; the using agency confirms permission; the airspace has been released to the controlling agency; the aircraft is on an approved ALTRV unless the area is an ATCAA; or the operation is offshore or oceanic and Chapter 8 procedures apply.

choices.text: Treat the using-agency permission as an exception and handle the flight accordingly.
Apply these avoidance procedures anyway because pilot-reported using-agency permission has no effect unless restated by the controlling agency.
Deny the operation automatically because pilots may never relay using-agency permission for entry into active special use airspace.
Apply the exception only if the airspace is an ATCAA, because MOAs and restricted areas are never covered by pilot-reported permission.

content.explanation: If the pilot informs ATC that permission has been obtained from the using agency to operate in the airspace, the special use airspace procedures are not applied as though no exception exists.

operations.problem: The flashcard back lists four distinct exception conditions in a single retrieval target: using-agency permission, airspace released to controlling agency, approved ALTRV, and offshore/oceanic operations. Each condition represents a separate operational rule with unique qualifying details. A single overloaded card prevents focused retrieval of any one condition.
operations.source_basis: Apply the procedures in this section to aircraft operating in proximity to special use, ATC-assigned airspace (ATCAA), and stationary ALTRVs unless the airspace is designated an alert area/controlled firing area or one of the following conditions exist: a. The pilot informs you that permission has been obtained from the using agency; b. The using agency informs you they have given permission; c. The restricted/warning area, MOA, ATCAA, or stationary ALTRV has been released to the controlling agency; d. The aircraft is on an approved ALTRV, unless the airspace area in question is an ATCAA.

match.front: When do the Chapter 9 special-use airspace procedures not apply?
match.back: They do not apply when the pilot already has using-agency permission, the using agency gives permission, the airspace has been released to the controlling agency, or the aircraft is on an approved ALTRV as specified.

replacements.front: How does using-agency permission affect the application of special use airspace procedures?
replacements.back: The procedures do not apply when the pilot informs ATC that permission has been obtained from the using agency, or when the using agency informs ATC they have given permission.

replacements.front: How does release to the controlling agency affect special use airspace procedures?
replacements.back: The procedures do not apply when the restricted area, warning area, MOA, ATCAA, or stationary ALTRV has been released to the controlling agency.

replacements.front: How does an approved ALTRV affect special use airspace procedures?
replacements.back: The procedures do not apply when the aircraft is on an approved ALTRV, unless the airspace area in question is an ATCAA.

match.question_text: Under 9-3-2, what altitude separation is used to separate nonparticipating aircraft from active SUA, ATCAAs, and stationary ALTRVs?

replacements.question_text: What altitude separation is used to separate nonparticipating aircraft from active special use airspace, ATCAAs, and stationary ALTRVs?
replacements.explanation: Unless an LOA or LOP provides otherwise, assign an altitude consistent with flight direction rules that is at least 500 feet above or below the upper or lower limit of the active airspace, or at least 1,000 feet when above FL290.

choices.text: At least 500 feet above or below the upper or lower limit, or 1,000 feet above FL290.
Exactly 1,000 feet above or below all SUA and ALTRV limits, regardless of altitude.
Any altitude consistent with flight direction, even if it touches the SUA vertical limits.
Only lateral separation is authorized; altitude separation is not used for active SUA or stationary ALTRVs.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 9-3-2' and the explanation cites 'Per 9-3-2b,' embedding paragraph-location scaffolding instead of teaching the operational separation requirement.
operations.source_basis: 9-3-2b requires radar separation of 3 miles from the peripheral boundary, or 6 miles at FL600 and above, with exceptions for certain prohibited, restricted, or warning areas established for security or hazardous activity containment where aircraft are vectored to remain clear of the boundary.

match.question_text: Under 9-3-2, what radar separation is normally provided from the peripheral boundary of special use airspace, an ATCAA, or a stationary ALTRV?

replacements.question_text: What radar separation is normally provided from the peripheral boundary of special use airspace, an ATCAA, or a stationary ALTRV?
replacements.explanation: Provide 3 miles of radar separation from the peripheral boundary, or 6 miles at FL600 and above, unless an exception applies. Some prohibited, restricted, or warning areas established for security reasons or to contain hazardous activities do not require the 3-mile or 6-mile buffer — for those areas, vector aircraft to remain clear of the peripheral boundary.

choices.text: 3 miles, or 6 miles at FL600 and above, from the peripheral boundary unless an exception applies.
5 miles from all peripheral boundaries at every altitude with no exceptions.
1 mile unless the area is restricted or warning airspace, in which case 3 miles applies.
No radar separation is used from the peripheral boundary if the aircraft is IFR.

operations.problem: This true/false item tests a verbatim sentence from the note ('VFR traffic is not prohibited from transiting stationary ALTRVs or transitional hazard areas') with paragraph-location scaffolding in the stem and an explanation that merely confirms the statement appears in the text. The operational distinction between VFR traffic and nonparticipating IFR aircraft is worth testing, but the format and explanation fail to teach it.
operations.source_basis: The note to 9-3-2 defines nonparticipating aircraft as those for which controllers have separation responsibility and which have not been authorized by the using agency, and states that VFR traffic is not prohibited from transiting stationary ALTRVs or transitional hazard areas.

match.question_text: Under 9-3-2, VFR traffic is not prohibited from transiting stationary ALTRVs or transitional hazard areas (THA).

replacements.question_text: How does the separation requirement for VFR traffic differ from nonparticipating IFR aircraft regarding stationary ALTRVs and transitional hazard areas?
replacements.explanation: Nonparticipating aircraft are those for which controllers have separation responsibility and which have not been authorized by the using agency to operate in or through the special use airspace, ATCAA, or stationary ALTRV. VFR traffic is not prohibited from transiting stationary ALTRVs or THAs, so controllers are not required to separate VFR aircraft from these areas the way they must separate nonparticipating IFR aircraft.

choices.text: Controllers must separate nonparticipating IFR aircraft from stationary ALTRVs and THAs, but VFR traffic is permitted to transit them.
Both VFR and IFR traffic are equally restricted from transiting stationary ALTRVs and THAs.
VFR traffic must be separated from stationary ALTRVs but may freely transit THAs.
Neither VFR nor nonparticipating IFR aircraft may transit stationary ALTRVs, but both may transit THAs.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 9-3-3' and the explanation begins with 'Per 9-3-3,' embedding paragraph-location scaffolding that detracts from the operational rule being taught.
operations.source_basis: 9-3-3 provides two options when an aircraft's route, track, or altitude may cause entry into active SUA or an ATCAA: instruct VFR-on-top at least 500 feet above or below the airspace limits, or clear via a routing that provides approved separation from the airspace.

match.question_text: Under 9-3-3, what may a controller instruct a VFR-on-top aircraft to do if its route, track, or altitude may cause it to enter active SUA or an ATCAA?

replacements.explanation: If the aircraft's route, track, or altitude may cause entry into active special use airspace or an ATCAA, the controller has two options: instruct the pilot to maintain VFR-on-top at least 500 feet above the upper limit or below the lower limit of the airspace, or clear the aircraft via a routing that provides approved separation from the airspace.

choices.text: Maintain VFR-on-top at least 500 feet above the upper limit or below the lower limit of the airspace, or accept a route that provides approved separation from it.
Remain exactly on the filed route through the airspace because VFR-on-top status automatically authorizes entry into active SUA and ATCAAs.
Descend into the airspace as long as the pilot reports the area in sight.
Cancel IFR immediately because VFR-on-top operations may never continue near active SUA or ATCAAs.

choices.text: No added altitude buffer is required; vector the aircraft to remain clear of the peripheral boundary.
At least 500 feet above or below the upper or lower limit of the area.
At least 1,000 feet above or below the upper or lower limit of the area.

replacements.question_text: A controller has coordinated transit of active special use airspace under an LOA with the using agency but cannot comply with the using agency's instructions. What should the controller do?
replacements.explanation: When a controller cannot comply with the using agency's instructions for a coordinated transit, the contingency is to treat the aircraft as a nonparticipating aircraft and apply the standard separation minima: altitude separation of at least 500 feet (1,000 feet above FL290) above or below the airspace limits, or radar separation of 3 miles (6 miles above FL600) from the peripheral boundary.

choices.text: Separate the aircraft from the active airspace using the standard altitude or radar separation minima that apply to nonparticipating aircraft.
Proceed with the transit regardless, because the LOA obligates compliance with the using agency's instructions even when they cannot be met.
Cancel the IFR clearance and require the pilot to transit VFR only.

Wait until the jump operation is complete because coordination is required only after the final parachutist is on the ground.
Coordinate only if the jump aircraft enters Class A airspace because routine parachute operations do not require interfacility coordination.
Coordinate only with the next radar sector and not with any other affected ATC facility.

content.explanation: Per 9-7-1, coordination occurs both prior to and at the end of each parachute jump or series of jumps affecting your area.

content.situation: A series of parachute jumps begins in your area and will end in an adjacent sector. Another controller wants to wait until the entire jump operation is over before telling the other sector anything.
content.para_context: Coordinate any pertinent information prior to and at the end of each parachute jump or series of jumps that begins or ends in your area of jurisdiction with other affected ATC facilities or sectors.

choices.text: Coordinate pertinent information before the jump operation and again at the end with the affected ATC facilities or sectors.
Wait until the jump operation is complete because coordination is required only after the final parachutist is on the ground.
Coordinate only if the jump aircraft enters Class A airspace because routine parachute operations do not require interfacility coordination.
Coordinate only with the next radar sector and not with any other affected ATC facility.

content.explanation: The controlling rule requires coordination of pertinent information both before the jump operation begins and again when it ends with all affected ATC facilities or sectors, not only after completion or with a single facility.

match.question_text: Under 9-7-2, what must be done with aircraft not participating in the jump operation?

replacements.question_text: In Class A, B, or C airspace, what must be done with aircraft that are not participating in a parachute jump operation?
replacements.explanation: The controlling rule requires controllers to separate nonparticipating aircraft from the airspace designated and authorized for the parachute jumping activity, not merely issue traffic advisories or allow transit under visual conditions.

choices.text: Separate them from the airspace authorized for the jumping activity.
Issue traffic advisories only, because separation is not provided around designated jump airspace.
Allow them to transit the jump airspace if the jump pilot reports the aircraft in sight.
Apply separation only if the jump aircraft is IFR and the other aircraft is VFR.

replacements.question_text: In Class A, B, or C airspace, where may parachute operations be authorized?
replacements.explanation: The controlling rule limits parachute operations in Class A, B, and C airspace to the airspace that has been specifically designated for the jumping activity, not anywhere within the facility's general controlled airspace.

choices.text: Only within airspace designated for the jumping activity.
Anywhere in the controlling facility's Class A, B, or C airspace once one jump block has been approved.
Anywhere the jump aircraft can remain in two-way communications with ATC.
Only inside restricted or warning areas that already exist on the chart.

content.question_text: What is required when authorizing parachute operations in Class A, Class B, or Class C airspace?

choices.text: Authorize them only within airspace designated for the jump activity and separate nonparticipating aircraft from that airspace.
Allow jumpers to drift outside the designated area as long as advisories are issued.
Treat all aircraft in the area as participating traffic until the last jumper lands.
Authorize the operation only after the jump pilot agrees to waive separation from nonparticipating aircraft.

content.explanation: Per 9-7-2, parachute operations in Class A, B, and C airspace are authorized only within the airspace designated for the jump, nonparticipating aircraft are separated from that airspace, and any needed conditions or restrictions may be imposed.

content.question_text: What is required when authorizing parachute operations in Class A, Class B, or Class C airspace?

choices.text: Authorize them only within airspace designated for the jump activity and separate nonparticipating aircraft from that airspace.
Allow jumpers to drift outside the designated area as long as advisories are issued.

Authorize the operation only after the jump pilot agrees to waive separation from nonparticipating aircraft.

content.explanation: The controlling rule for Class A, B, and C airspace requires three actions: authorize parachute operations only within the airspace designated for the jump, separate nonparticipating aircraft from that designated airspace, and impose any conditions or restrictions the controller judges necessary for safety.

match.question_text: Under 9-7-3, parachute jumping in or into Class D airspace with a functioning control tower is authorized with respect to what?

replacements.question_text: In Class D airspace with a functioning control tower, parachute jumping is authorized with respect to what?
replacements.explanation: The controlling rule for Class D airspace requires authorizing parachute jumping with respect to known or observed traffic, not based solely on weather, the jump aircraft's requested route, or only IFR traffic considerations.

choices.text: Known or observed traffic.
Only IFR traffic because VFR traffic is responsible for its own separation from jump operations.
Only the jump aircraft's requested route and altitude because traffic is handled after the jump begins.
Weather conditions alone, because traffic is not considered until parachutists are clear of the aircraft.

replacements.explanation: The controlling rule requires issuing advisory information to both the jump aircraft and nonparticipating aircraft as necessary for the safe conduct of the jump operation, not to only one party or only under specific flight-rule conditions.

Only the jump aircraft because nonparticipating aircraft are expected to avoid the area without advisories.
Only aircraft operating IFR because VFR pilots do not receive jump-operation advisories.

content.question_text: How are requests for parachute jumping in Class D airspace with a functioning control tower handled?

choices.text: Authorize the jump with respect to known or observed traffic and issue advisory information as necessary to both jump and nonparticipating aircraft.
Approve the jump automatically and rely on the pilot to avoid all known traffic.
Deny the jump unless the tower can stop all other traffic in the Class D airspace.
Handle the request the same way as uncontrolled Class E airspace with ATIS-only advisories.

content.question_text: How are requests for parachute jumping in Class D airspace with a functioning control tower handled?

choices.text: Authorize the jump with respect to known or observed traffic and issue advisory information as necessary to both jump and nonparticipating aircraft.
Approve the jump automatically and rely on the pilot to avoid all known traffic.
Deny the jump unless the tower can stop all other traffic in the Class D airspace.
Handle the request the same way as uncontrolled Class E airspace with ATIS-only advisories.

content.explanation: The controlling rule for Class D airspace with a functioning tower requires two actions: authorize the parachute jump with respect to known or observed traffic, and issue advisory information as necessary to both the jump aircraft and nonparticipating aircraft for the safe conduct of the operation.

replacements.explanation: The controlling rule requires that the traffic advisory to the jump aircraft include the aircraft type, altitude, and direction of flight of all known traffic that will transit the airspace where the jump will be conducted, not merely the nearest aircraft or the jump's own parameters.

Only by NOTAM because real-time broadcasting is not authorized for jump operations.
